title: Acute gout: first-line is NSAID
vignette: Obese diabetic with sudden nocturnal first-MTP pain; synovial fluid shows needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals; normal creatinine. Best initial treatment?
whatMatters: Acute gout flare = anti-inflammatory (NSAID first-line). Urate-lowering drugs are for chronic prevention and can worsen a flare.
strategy: Acute = put out the fire (NSAID/colchicine/steroids); chronic = lower urate (allopurinol/febuxostat/probenecid).

eliminations.correctIf: Patient had aspirin-exacerbated asthma and the question was about leukotriene pathway blockade
Patient had rheumatoid arthritis or psoriatic arthritis (not acute crystalline gout)
Patient was in gout remission and the question asked about PREVENTING future attacks via urate lowering

eliminations.why: Doesn't treat acute attack; can prolong it. Trap.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient was already stable on chronic allopurinol (continuation during acute flare may be acceptable)

reasoning: Needle-shaped negatively birefringent crystals + first-MTP = acute gout
First-line = NSAID (COX inhibitor); normal creatinine makes it safe -> A
mechanism: Urate crystals -> neutrophil-driven inflammation. First-line acute Tx: NSAIDs (inhibit COX); alternatives colchicine, glucocorticoids. Don't start urate-lowering therapy during a flare.

firstAid.location: Rheum/Pharm > acute vs chronic gout therapy; crystal ID

memoryHook: Put out the FIRE first, lower the URATE later.
